questions.q: Which statement best defines nuclear fusion?
options: A heavy nucleus splitting into two lighter nuclei, releasing energy
Two light nuclei joining to form a single heavier nucleus that is more tightly bound, releasing energy
A nucleus emitting an alpha particle to become more stable
Electrons being stripped from atoms to form a plasma
questions.explanation: Fusion joins two light (small mass number) nuclei into a heavier, more tightly bound nucleus, releasing energy. Splitting a heavy nucleus (A) is fission, and alpha emission (C) is radioactive decay.

questions.q: Using the binding-energy-per-nucleon curve, why does fusing light nuclei release energy?
options: Light nuclei are at the iron peak, so fusing them releases the most energy
The product sits lower on the curve, so it has less binding energy
The product sits higher on the curve, closer to iron, so it is more tightly bound and has less mass than the reactants; that mass defect is released as energy
Mass is destroyed and converted to pure energy with no change in binding
questions.explanation: Light nuclei sit low on the steep left of the curve. Their fused product sits higher, nearer iron-56, with greater binding energy per nucleon, so it is more tightly bound and lighter than the reactants. That mass defect is released via \(E = mc^2\). Mass is converted, not destroyed (D).

questions.q: Why does nuclear fusion require extremely high temperatures?
options: To melt the nuclei so they can flow together
To give the positively charged nuclei enough kinetic energy to overcome the Coulomb repulsion and approach within range of the strong nuclear force
To weaken the strong nuclear force so the nuclei can merge
To strip electrons away so gravity can pull the nuclei together
questions.explanation: Both nuclei are positively charged and repel via the long-range Coulomb force. Since temperature measures average kinetic energy, an extremely high temperature gives the nuclei enough kinetic energy to beat this Coulomb barrier and get within about \(10^{-15}\ \mathrm{m}\), where the short-range strong force can bind them.

questions.q: Besides high temperature, why is high pressure (density) also required for sustained fusion?
options: To increase the strength of the Coulomb barrier
To slow the nuclei down so they can be captured
To pack nuclei close together so collisions occur frequently enough to sustain the reaction
To cool the plasma and prevent it from escaping
questions.explanation: High temperature makes each collision energetic enough; high pressure and density make collisions frequent enough to sustain the reaction. In a star, gravity supplies the crushing pressure across its huge mass.

questions.q: Which force overcomes the Coulomb repulsion to bind two nuclei together once they are close enough?
options: The gravitational force
The weak nuclear force
The strong nuclear force, which is attractive but acts only over a very short range
The electromagnetic force
questions.explanation: The strong nuclear force is powerfully attractive but acts only over about \(10^{-15}\ \mathrm{m}\). Gravity is far too feeble at the nuclear scale, and the weak force mediates beta decay rather than binding nucleons.

questions.q: In the reaction \({}^{2}_{1}\mathrm{H} + {}^{3}_{1}\mathrm{H} \to {}^{4}_{2}\mathrm{He} + X\), what is the particle \(X\)?
options: A proton
A neutron
A positron
An alpha particle
questions.explanation: Balance the numbers: mass \(2 + 3 = 5\), and \({}^{4}\mathrm{He}\) accounts for 4, leaving 1; charge \(1 + 1 = 2\), and helium accounts for 2, leaving 0. A mass-1, charge-0 particle is a neutron.

questions.q: The deuterium-tritium fusion reaction has a mass defect of about \(0.0189\ \mathrm{u}\). Using \(1\ \mathrm{u} \leftrightarrow 931.5\ \mathrm{MeV}\), the energy released per event is closest to:
options: 1.8 MeV
17.6 MeV
200 MeV
questions.explanation: \(E = \Delta m \times 931.5 = 0.0189 \times 931.5 \approx 17.6\ \mathrm{MeV}\) per D-T event. The 200 MeV figure (C) is a uranium fission, not this fusion. Remember not to divide by \(c^2\) as well — it is built into 931.5.

questions.q: How do fusion, fission and chemical combustion compare in energy released PER UNIT MASS of fuel?
options: Chemical > fission > fusion
Fission > fusion > chemical
Fusion > fission > chemical (both nuclear processes vastly exceed chemical)
They are all roughly equal
questions.explanation: Per unit mass the order is fusion > fission ≫ chemical. Although a single fission event (~200 MeV) exceeds a single fusion event (~17.6 MeV), fusion involves few nucleons on the steepest part of the curve, so per nucleon it wins. Chemical burning only rearranges electrons (~eV per atom).

questions.q: What is the iron limit, and why does it exist?
options: Iron is too heavy to fuse, so stars avoid making it

Fusion releases energy only up to iron-56 (the curve's peak); fusing beyond iron would produce a less tightly bound, heavier product and consume energy, so iron is the heaviest element built by ordinary fusion
Iron absorbs all the neutrons in a star, halting fusion
Beyond iron, the Coulomb barrier disappears, so fusion stops being needed
questions.explanation: Iron-56 is at the top of the binding-energy-per-nucleon curve. Fusing past iron yields a product lower on the curve (less tightly bound, more massive than the reactants), which requires energy input rather than releasing it. So fusion in a star releases energy only up to iron, making iron the heaviest element it can build this way.

questions.q: Why is sustained, controlled fusion not yet a viable energy source on Earth, whereas stars manage it readily?
options: There is not enough hydrogen fuel available on Earth
Fusion produces too much long-lived radioactive waste to be safe
Confining matter at the required extreme temperature and density is extremely difficult on Earth, while a star's own gravity confines it for free
The strong nuclear force does not operate on Earth
questions.explanation: Fusion needs matter at enormous temperature and density held together long enough to sustain the reaction. A star's immense gravity supplies this confinement for free; on Earth there is no material container (anything that hot vaporises), so reactors must use magnetic confinement, and sustained net-positive fusion has not yet been achieved. Fusion fuel is actually abundant and its waste is comparatively clean.
